report:
## Your Health Assessment

Hi Ryan, thanks for taking the time to complete this. A few clear patterns stand out, and what's interesting is how connected they are. The brain fog, the low energy, and the digestive issues you're experiencing likely aren't separate problems. Let's look at what's going on.

### Your Gut Is Likely Driving Your Brain Fog

You're dealing with bloating, constipation, diarrhea, and food sensitivities all at once. That combination is a strong signal of gut dysbiosis, an imbalance of intestinal bacteria. Here's what matters most for your goals: [dysbiosis creates a pro-inflammatory environment that can directly impair brain function](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4879184/) through multiple pathways, including the vagus nerve and inflammatory signaling that reaches the central nervous system. In other words, your brain fog and the bloating aren't a coincidence. They're connected at the root.

### Your Eating Pattern Is Working Against You

This one is important. Skipping breakfast, working through lunch on a protein bar, and then loading up at dinner means your body is running on stress hormones for most of the day. Cortisol and adrenaline are keeping you upright when food isn't available, and that "consistently low" energy you described is what that feels like. Research shows that [habitually skipping breakfast is associated with a disrupted cortisol rhythm and elevated HPA axis activity](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25545767/). Then when you eat that large dinner, those stress hormones finally drop, and you crash. That post-meal energy dip you reported isn't caused by eating. It's revealing that stress hormones were the only thing holding your energy up in the first place.

This pattern also feeds directly back into your gut issues. Chronic stress suppresses digestive function, slows motility, and weakens the gut's immune defenses, making dysbiosis worse.

### Cold Extremities Point to a Sluggish Metabolism

Frequently cold fingers and toes, combined with brain fog and consistently low energy, is a classic picture of reduced thyroid function. Your thyroid sets the pace for your entire metabolism, including how well your gut moves, how efficiently your brain runs, and how much heat your body produces. If thyroid activity is low, everything slows down: motility drops (hello, constipation and bloating), cognitive sharpness fades, and energy stays flat regardless of what you do.

### The Connecting Thread

What we see in your assessment is a pattern we encounter often: undereating during the day drives stress hormones up, which suppresses gut function and metabolic rate over time. The gut becomes a source of inflammation that saps energy and clouds thinking. And a sluggish metabolism makes the gut even less capable of handling what's thrown at it. Each piece reinforces the others.

The encouraging part? Because these issues share common roots, addressing them doesn't require a dozen separate interventions. It requires the right ones, targeted at the right level.

---

A quiz can surface patterns, but it can only go so far. If you'd like to explore what's driving your brain fog and low energy in more detail, we offer a free consultation with someone on our team who understands these connections deeply. No cost, no obligation. It's just a conversation to see if we can help. You'll find the booking button just below.
